column2: — If you want to drive someone around, better drive Miyechka to some kind of sport, so she doesn't suffer.
— I'll suffer either way, — Miyechka spoke up.
The three of them roamed the vegetable garden planted with pumpkins.
Lilichka was taking photos, lining up Marta, Miyechka and the grandmother in a row against the backdrop of the dug-out holes.
The three of them posed: Miyechka with the face of a martyr, Marta in profile so that her pregnant belly would show, and the grandmother sat on the little stool, her hands folded in her lap.
Lilichka clicked away, slightly adjusting the grandmother's hands, turning Marta's belly a few degrees, and, sensitively attuned to her sister, did not touch Miyechka.
Turning once again a few degrees on Lilichka's instructions, Marta said:
— He came to India looking for enlightenment, the idiot.
And he found me and made me two children.
Granny, were there twins in our family line?
The grandmother sat for a while in silence, asking no unnecessary questions, because everyone knew that with Marta you just had to wait for the right moment, and said:
— In our family all the women gave birth one at a time.
— I thought as much, that this couldn't be from our side.
To give birth to several at once, like cats, — said Marta.

Lilichka's phone, meanwhile, kept ringing all the time, while the currants grew still blacker, while the sour cherries grew bigger, and the starlings were already flying in to check whether the sweet cherries had ripened.
— Who's fighting?
— Lilichka shouted into the receiver.
— Marko?
Put him on the phone!
Apologize!
— she exclaimed, and then in a thin, tiny little voice hurriedly begged the implacable Marko to stop fighting.
Miyechka would then run up to her sister and whisper to her:
— Don't say anything about the children fighting.
Don't let Marta get scared.
— She has no way back now, — Lilichka chuckled.
— Besides, once she gives birth, the hormones are supposed to kick in.
— Our Marta didn't get any hormones at birth, — said Miyechka.
— What's going to kick in with her?
It's Marta, after all.
— If the Lord gave her to conceive, then He'll give her hormones afterward too, — said the grandmother.
— Marta!
— Miyechka called out.
— Come over here, grandmother's holding forth about creationism!
— You mustn't go so far, — the grandmother shook her head.
— One must believe in God, then people are happier and don't drink themselves to ruin.
— And that one you said drank himself to ruin from taking communion?
— asked Miyechka.
— How many wise things I've told you, Solomiya, and this is the one you remembered.
They still hadn't found out about the children's father even by the time the pumpkins had put out two little leaves each, from which shoots were just about to appear.

Marta, whose belly had by now grown to the size of a small watermelon, together with Lilichka, who scurried about the garden on her thin little legs with the camera, photographed a hundred two-leaf seedlings.
Miyechka and the grandmother sat under the enormous, spreading walnut tree.
A jay sat in the walnut tree and screeched.
Miyechka did the mechanical work, being in charge of numbering each individual pumpkin.
— Mom?
— Solomiya said into the phone, holding it out in front of her.
— Hello, can you hear me?
— came from the other end.
— Everything's FINE with us, — the grandmother shouted from under the walnut tree.
— Did you hear?
Where are you?
— Solomiya said into the phone.
— I'm somewhere in Greece, all the islands here end in 'os,' I don't know where I am.
What's going on with you?
— The girls are growing pumpkins, — the grandmother shouted so loudly that you could probably hear her even across the ravine, beyond which the neighbors already lived.
— I'll show you now, — Solomiya swept the phone around in a panorama, showing the garden.
On their knees, Marta and Lilichka were photographing the pumpkins.
After a while Marta came over to the walnut tree and said:
— Don't tell my mother that I'm pregnant.
— That wasn't your mother on the phone, — the grandmother blurted out at once, though she herself had already, yesterday in the raspberry as evening fell, told the news to Marta's mother by phone.
And besides, Marta had heard it perfectly well, lying in her own bedroom.
She simply could never deny herself a bit of conspiracy, because then there was less talk about her relationship with her mother.
The latter had given birth to Marta as a vexing substitute for a boy, since she had dreamed of having only sons.
She had a son who was born weak, pale and sickly, and in her heart she resented Marta for always being healthy, strong and rosy-cheeked, when it was the son who ought to have been like that.
— She was simply born that way, — said the grandmother, who believed that upbringing didn't count for much and that it wasn't as easy to inflict trauma on a child as people think nowadays.
— You're not giving birth to dough, after all, — she would say.
— You're giving birth to a person.
And however it was born, so it will be, which is why all these therapies of yours, where they go hunting for everyone who ever wronged you in life, are just money thrown away…
Lilichka would tell how all her friends went to therapy and paid a hundred and twenty dollars an hour.
— Just think how much salmon you could buy for that?
— the grandmother would ask, for she liked to measure human goods in kilograms of salmon, which, as she believed, had brought her to ninety-six years of age with a clear head and relative health.
Lilichka would sometimes argue and say that therapy can bring you back to life.
— Imagine, child, if I'd started digging around in the traumas of my childhood?
— the grandmother would then ask.
— What would have become of me?
My tears would never have dried, and you, my little ones, would never have seen my eyes.
— Those were different times, — Lilichka would retort.
— The times were different, but the people were the same.
— Li, — Miyechka would cut in, — if you enjoy going to therapy, then go, or are you trying to send grandmother there?
— I just want to justify those crazy expenses to her.
— Right, especially if you measure them in salmon.
The pumpkins grew, and Marta's belly grew along with them.
From time to time they weeded, so the pumpkins would stay photogenic for Lilichka's photos, and the grandmother also wouldn't let Marta weed, though Marta resisted.
— Your great-grandfather, back when women gave birth out in the field, hired outsiders to do the weeding when your great-grandmother was expecting, — said the grandmother.
— And how many of her children died?
— Marta would toss back in reply, leaning on her hoe.
The grandmother would then take her little cane and walk slowly out of the garden, muttering:
— Impossible with this girl, impossible…
Lusya

One morning, when they were all sitting outside — Marta was in a yellow shirt with red poppies, and you could see her from beyond the horizon, so caustically bright were the colors, while Miyechka sat with her feet up on the table, because over the last decade or so the grandmother had already come to terms with the fact that she would never give up this habit, and only asked that she keep her feet under the table if anyone came by — Lilichka's phone rang.
She got up from the table, gave a swish of the full skirt she'd had since the seventh grade, and walked off under the oaks.
— Now who could be calling her?
— asked the grandmother.
— You can see better, what's showing up on her screen?
— Ivan called, — said Miyechka.
— Which one?
— Marta spoke up; she was just then slicing apples into pieces and crunching on them.
— The same one.
— But wasn't her other one called Ivan too?
— asked Marta.
— The other one was called Aiven.
And this one is Ivan.
Ivan had taken Lilichka's heart captive when she was still a very young, fair-skinned, tender little bird.
With a little head that turned on a delicate neck, with little arms that grew from marble, slightly bony little shoulders, with long, slender legs.
Ivan played the piano and won Lilichka over with his singing and his laughter.
He played and laughed, laughed and played.
The grandfather, who was still alive then, said that this was no real man.
But Lilichka looked straight through the grandfather, and through everyone, and even through herself, and saw only him, Ivan.
Ivan had gray-blue eyes with light-brown, terribly fluffy lashes and sang in a tenor that made women's hearts swoon.
Toward him flocked tall, red-lipped, slender women with high, full breasts, but Ivan chose little, tender Lilichka.
He chose her and said: 'I'll marry her!'.
He went about with his shirt open, his tanned chest gleaming and his forelock tousled.
'The world has never seen such a fatally handsome man,' thought Lilichka.

— Careful, child, — the grandmother would say. — A man can marry a little bird like you, light as a feather, give her a baby, and then that little bird becomes such a burden that he has to drown her in the river — an American tragedy that isn't only American…
— It's just that no one writes about happy married life!
— Lilichka would object.
Miyechka quickly found common ground with her sister's fiancé, but he did not seem attractive to her.

Ivan taught Miyechka to play Love of My Life on the keyboard, and even when Lilichka divorced Ivan, Miyechka said: 'You know, he brought us a lot of good — a son for you, and he taught me to play Love of My Life.'
But Lilichka herself had no regrets either.

After the divorce, when Lilichka sat down across from him in the kitchen and said that it was probably time for them to go their separate ways, Ivan began to drift around the taverns, where he played in the evenings and danced through the nights.
But then he suddenly became an engineer, got married, and began to wear neat sweaters and shave clean.
It turned out that all those years he had needed glasses, and only now had he begun to wear them and started to see the world clearly and rationally.
Lilichka had thought that without her Ivan would be lost — would carouse, fall into depression, stop shaving and getting his hair cut.
She had been secretly saving up money for a psychotherapist for him.

When everything turned out the opposite way and the divorce made a sensible man of Ivan, she used that money to rent a yellow car for two weeks, blew out a tire somewhere in the wild steppe, and was rescued from there by Aiven.
He photographed old factories.
His lashes were just like Ivan's.
Lilichka came back to the table, where Solomiya, Marta and the grandmother sat, and said:
— Ivan called.
Everyone turned their heads toward her, and she went on:
— He said he'd bring a child.
— What child?
— asked the grandmother.
— His own.
— You mean yours?
— No, ours isn't with him.
— Then which one?
— He had a little girl a couple of years ago, — said Lilichka.
— A girl?
— asked the grandmother.
— A little girl named Lusya.
— Somehow that's not one of our names, — said Miyechka.
— So he's going to bring this Lusya?
— asked the grandmother.
— He'll bring her.
— And what do we need her for?
— Marta inquired.
— He's got some serious problems, — replied Lilichka.
— I said — you come over, and we'll talk it through here.
— Well, it's good that at least it's a girl, — said Marta.
— A girl, at least.
Ivan arrived the next morning, when everyone was just beginning to wake up.
Miyechka, still in her nightgown, stood outside drinking tea with a chocolate sweet.
She needed glucose after the night — that was how she justified this ritual.
Ivan stood beyond the gate.
The grandmother slowly came out onto the threshold and said:
— Miyechka, we'll have to let this man into our female kingdom.
He has a little girl in his arms.
Miyechka, covering with her falling hair the breasts that showed through the nightgown, came up to the gate.
— Hello, Solomiya, — said Ivan.
— Come in, — said Miyechka, took him by the shoulder and reached for the little hand of the girl, who sat meekly in his arm.
The little girl had thin white hair, big eyes, very fine little lips, and did not pull her hand away when Miyechka took hold of it.
She bore a frightening resemblance to that imaginary child Miyechka sometimes led by the hand around the city.
She reached out to the unfamiliar woman and went easily into her arms.
The little blonde sat in Miyechka's arm and looked at her dad.
Then she began to finger Miyechka's hair, still uncombed from the night — it curled at the ends.
With the child in her arms she walked on ahead.
Ivan still stood beyond the gate; Miyechka turned to him and said:
— Come on.
And they set off toward the house.
Miyechka carried the little girl, who silently fingered her hair.
— Good morning, — Marta stood on the threshold.
She reached out to the child, and then, over Miyechka's head, nodded to Ivan.
He stood there at a loss and ill at ease.
— Ivanko, will you have breakfast?
— the grandmother spoke up, peering out of the house.
He came up to her and put his arms around her shoulders.
— I'll have breakfast, — and then he turned to Miyechka, who still stood in her nightgown with the child in her arms, and pointed at the girl with the words: — Grandma Teya, this is Lusya.
— She looks very much like you, Ivan, — said the grandmother.
Later, when Marta and the grandmother were busy with breakfast, while Ivan, Miyechka and Lusya waited outside, Marta said:
— Granny, the child doesn't look anything like him at all.
— Marta, you must always tell men that their children hypothetically look like them.
Once everyone was sitting outside, Lusya wouldn't leave Miyechka's side, kept nestling up to her.
— She didn't sleep in the car, — said Ivan.
— Maybe she's sleepy.
Let her doze off, — said Miyechka and drew Lusya close.
But the girl still wouldn't let go of her hair, fingering it and fingering it.
A certain tension could still be felt, because Lilichka still hadn't come out, and Ivan was worried.
But before long she appeared in the doorway.
Dressed in a greenish dress with thin little straps, with a sports jacket thrown over it.
The same little bird's head, the same smile.
